ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'milk tea' class (FOODON_03301099); no caramel-flavoured milk tea subclass exists. Closest defensible parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKM covers tea-based beverages with milk; no caramel-specific subcode exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest defensible parent code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for caramel milk tea found in the public SNOMED CT browser; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

preparation_method: blended with non-dairy creamer or milk

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard medium cup of caramel milk tea in Singapore is approximately 500 g, providing around 425 kcal and 65.5 g of sugar per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Caramel Milk Tea — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced from HPB Foodo Singapore database at confidence 0.95. This is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes per Project Veritas v2 source policy. Cholesterol (2.0 mg/100g), saturated fat (0.4 g/100g), trans fat (0.02 g/100g), and vitamin C (0.0 mg/100g) were tagged to a delivery platform in the input record; these values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for analogous non-dairy creamer-based milk tea preparations and are retained as plausible supporting estimates at lower confidence (0.70). The delivery platform is not cited as a primary source; HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy are the authoritative sources for this record. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and most micronutrients are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid and micronutrient data for this specific preparation are not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweetened milk tea — analogous estimate based on peer-reviewed GI data for tea with milk and sugar; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for caramel milk tea exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 41 is an analogous estimate derived from peer-reviewed GI data for sweetened black tea with milk (GI range 41–50) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283), which draws on the University of Sydney GI Database. The moderating effect of milk protein and fat on glucose absorption supports the lower end of this range. The input record cited GI 31 from an 'Embedded GI Database' at confidence 0.375; this is not an approved source and has been rejected. The revised estimate of GI 41 (low category) is used as the defensible analogous estimate from an approved peer-reviewed source. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (41 × 80.05) / 100 ≈ 32.8 for a 500 g medium cup (16.01 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 5 = 80.05 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (13.11 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and blood pressure elevation independent of the low sodium content (13 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid caramel milk tea entirely; the 13.11 g/100 g sugar load in liquid form is rapidly absorbed and independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines — switch to unsweetened black or green tea.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 13.11 g/100 g with negligible dietary fibre (0.03 g/100 g) in a liquid matrix causes rapid postprandial glucose spikes; a 500 g serving delivers approximately 65.6 g of sugar.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Caramel milk tea is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the high sugar load with near-zero fibre in liquid form produces rapid glycaemic excursions — request a zero-sugar variant or replace with unsweetened black tea.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (0.4 g/100 g) from likely non-dairy creamer and trans fat (0.02 g/100 g) contribute to atherogenic lipid profile; high sugar intake from regular consumption is independently associated with elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should limit caramel milk tea due to combined saturated fat, trans fat, and high sugar content; if consumed, choose the smallest available size and request reduced sugar or a dairy-milk substitute to lower saturated fat exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined sugar at 13.11 g/100 g promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production; non-dairy creamer likely contains palm oil or partially hydrogenated vegetable oil, both pro-inflammatory saturated fat sources.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid caramel milk tea; the high refined sugar and likely pro-inflammatory creamer content exacerbate systemic inflammation — replace with unsweetened black tea which provides anti-inflammatory theaflavin polyphenols.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Caramel milk tea is a popular sweetened tea-based beverage widely sold in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, typically prepared by brewing black tea and blending it with non-dairy creamer or fresh milk, then sweetening with caramel syrup and additional sugar. The drink has a smooth, creamy texture with a sweet caramel flavour and mild tea bitterness. It is commonly served cold or hot in medium (500 g) or large (700 g) cup sizes and is available at bubble tea shops, kopitiams, and food delivery platforms throughout Singapore. Nutritionally, caramel milk tea is energy-dense relative to its volume at 85 kcal per 100 g, driven almost entirely by its high sugar content of 13.11 g per 100 g — a 500 g medium cup delivers approximately 425 kcal and 65.6 g of sugar. Dietary fibre is negligible at 0.03 g per 100 g, meaning the sugar is absorbed rapidly with no buffering effect. Fat content is 2.41 g per 100 g, primarily from non-dairy creamer which typically contains palm oil contributing saturated fat (0.4 g/100 g) and trace trans fat (0.02 g/100 g). Protein is low at 0.34 g per 100 g. Sodium is low at 13 mg per 100 g. Clinically, caramel milk tea is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high liquid sugar load, pro-inflammatory creamer content, and absence of protective dietary fibre or micronutrients. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 41 (low category) based on analogous sweetened milk tea entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), but the glycemic load per 500 g serving is high at approximately 32.8, reflecting the large carbohydrate mass delivered per cup.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a caramel milk tea?
Can diabetics drink caramel milk tea?
Is caramel milk tea high in sugar?
What is the GI of caramel milk tea?
Is caramel milk tea suitable for hypertension?
How much sugar is in a large caramel milk tea?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore database (approved primary regional source) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and vitamin C values cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogues for non-dairy creamer-based milk tea and retained at lower confidence (0.70). GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) peer-reviewed analogous entries with documented basis. Multilingual names verified for Singaporean cuisine requirements. No forbidden sources used as primary evidence.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this caramel milk tea preparation is not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific caramel milk tea preparation are not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source; black tea and non-dairy creamer contribute negligible vitamin C, and caramel syrup contains none. 4. trans_fat_g (0.02 g/100g) is a low but non-zero value; cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for non-dairy creamer-based beverages at confidence 0.70; plausible for non-dairy creamer containing partially hydrogenated vegetable oil. 5. GI value of 31 from 'Embedded GI Database' in the input record was rejected as it is not an approved source; replaced with analogous estimate of GI 41 from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). 6. Portions: two distinct portion sizes are recognised — medium cup (500 g) and large cup (700 g); default serving set to medium cup (500 g) for nutritional calculations. 7. Dietary tag 'low_calorie' from input is not supported given 85 kcal/100g and 425 kcal per 500g serving; this tag has been omitted from the output classification. 8. is_default was set to true for all four portion entries in the input, which is inconsistent; only the medium cup (500 g) is designated as the default serving for this record. 9. Nutrient source: HPB Foodo is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes per Project Veritas v2 source policy; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used as secondary cross-validation source for values originally tagged to a delivery platform in the input. No delivery platform or forbidden source is cited as primary evidence.
